title: Re-Indexing on Embedding Model Upgrades
concept: Why upgrading the embedding model requires re-embedding and re-indexing the entire corpus.

exercises.prompt: How do we typically measure the recall of a vector database to ensure search quality hasn't dropped?

options.text: By calculating how fast the query database returns results (p99 latency).
By comparing the returned search results against a curated ground-truth set.
By checking the total storage size of the vector database index.

exercises.explain: Recall is measured by comparing search results against a manually verified ground-truth set of relevant documents.

cards.title: Upgrading Your Model
cards.body: What happens when a better, smarter embedding model is released? You want to upgrade to get better search results, but you can't just plug it in. Let's see why upgrading requires a complete system refresh.

cards.title: Re-Indexing Explained
cards.body: When you upgrade an embedding model, the meaning of its output coordinates changes. Because old and new embeddings speak different 'languages,' you must re-generate vectors for your entire collection and build a brand-new index.

cards.title: The New Map Analogy
cards.body: Imagine swapping out a map of Paris for a map of Tokyo, but trying to use your old coordinates to find a bakery. The numbers point to a completely different layout! Upgrading an embedding model changes the coordinate system entirely.

cards.title: What Happens If You Mix Them
cards.body: If you search 'rental car' using a new model against an old index, you might get results about 'baking bread.' The new query vector points to a place in the new coordinate space that doesn't align with the old vectors.

cards.title: No Simple Translations
cards.body: You might think you can just map or translate old embeddings to fit the new model's dimensions. In reality, because models represent concepts differently, translation attempts fail; you must run the source text through the new model.

cards.title: Comparing Vector Spaces
cards.body: Different models represent the same word with entirely different numbers and dimensions, making direct comparison impossible.

exercises.prompt: Why can't you query an index built with 'Model A' using a query vector generated by 'Model B'?

options.text: The database will automatically delete the index to protect itself.
The coordinate spaces are different, leading to completely nonsensical match results.
Model B vectors are always twice as large as Model A vectors.
Vector databases only allow one model name to be typed in the settings.

exercises.explain: If you mix models, a query vector from Model B will have coordinates that mean something entirely different in the space created by Model A, leading to random and incorrect search results.

exercises.prompt: True or False: If two different embedding models both output vectors of size 768, you can safely mix their embeddings in the same index.
exercises.explain: Even if the vector size (dimension) is exactly the same, different models structure their coordinate spaces differently, meaning you must still re-index.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank to describe the relationship between embeddings from different models.
exercises.sentence: Embeddings created by two different models are completely incompatible for similarity search.
exercises.explain: Because different embedding models map text to different vector spaces, their outputs are completely incompatible with each other.

exercises.prompt: Identify the AI-generated explanation of model upgrades.

options.text: A database index is a data structure that improves the speed of data retrieval operations.
Vector similarity search calculates the distance between two arrays of floating-point numbers.
Upgrading the model alters the semantic topography, rendering the old coordinates mismatched.

exercises.explain: Option C is generated by an AI assistant attempting to explain vector space misalignment during model upgrades, while the others are standard database definitions.

exercises.prompt: Your team decides to upgrade from an older 384-dimension embedding model to a state-of-the-art 1536-dimension model to improve search quality. What is the absolute first thing you must do before querying your vector database with this new model?

options.text: Apply a mathematical offset to translate the old coordinates to the new ones.
Re-embed all source documents with the new model and rebuild the index.
Only embed new incoming queries with the new model while leaving the index as-is.
Keep the old index and use a standard translation matrix without re-embedding.

exercises.explain: Because the new model maps concepts to a completely different multi-dimensional space, old embeddings cannot be compared to new ones. You must run the entire document collection through the new model to recreate the index from scratch.
